The model

Vintage 2026-07  ·  Built 2026-07-11 13:01Z  ·  ← back to the forecast dashboard

The production model in one line: a 28-variable Bayesian VAR in log levels (CBO lineage), estimated on public FRED data from 1986Q1 under an order-invariant stochastic-volatility engine with a fixed Minnesota + sum-of-coefficients prior, publishing five forecast objects each month. This page is the full description and development record behind that sentence: the pipeline, the model written out symbol by symbol, what goes in and what comes out, every design decision with its evidence, and how the model evolved from the CBO base. It is meant to be readable top to bottom by someone new to the project with a basic time-series background; the interactive BVAR course teaches the underlying concepts in depth, and the forecast dashboard shows the current output.

The model at a glanceThe model, written outInside stage 2What makes up the modelWhat comes outTwo charts that explain a lotEstimation & prior decisionsVariable-set variantsHow the model got here

The model at a glance

The pipeline from data to published forecasts. Every decision card below is pinned to one of these six stages — click a stage (here or in the filter row) to see only its decisions.
Two things worth holding onto. First, stages 2 and 3 do different jobs that are easy to conflate: the prior (stage 2) restrains the coefficients, while the engine (stage 3) never moves coefficients directly — its stochastic volatility decides how loudly each historical quarter speaks, so wild quarters like 2020 are down-weighted automatically (no Covid dummies needed). Second, the prior and the sum-of-coefficients machinery together produce the signature property of stage 4 — every posterior draw sits just above the unit root (histogram below) — which is correct for trending data and explains several downstream choices. The next two sections make this concrete: first the model written out in symbols, then the prior stage taken apart piece by piece.

The model, written out

The complete production model, in five blocks. Nothing is elided: every distribution below is the one in the estimating code, and the two tables at the end inventory every quantity — the ∼12,800 numbers estimation learns and the handful of dials we set. Amber boxes are the alternatives that were tried, written in the same notation. Each block: the object in words, a picture, then the maths.

(1) One equation generates the data. 1 4

The object first: a vector autoregression is nothing more than 28 regression equations stacked together — one per variable — where the regressors of every equation are the same: everybody's values over the last four quarters. Here is the whole thing as shapes:

Each navy column is the data at one date; each green grid is a 28×28 block of lag weights; the red column is the surprise. Reading one white stripe across gives one ordinary regression: variable i today = weighted sum of everything's recent past + an intercept + a surprise. In symbols:

(1)

where each yt holds the 28 series after two fixed transforms — quantities and price indices enter as (so a difference of 1 ≈ a 1% change) and interest rates as (a convention inherited from the CBO original). With p = 4 lags, the first usable observation is 1987Q1, so estimation sees T = 157 quarters. Two facts to carry forward. Counting unknowns: c and the four B's hold 28 + 4×28×28 = 3,164 lag weights, and the engine below adds ∼9,600 more — against 157×28 ≈ 4,400 data points. Estimation without outside discipline is hopeless; blocks (3) and (4) supply it. And forecasting is just iteration: feed in the last four quarters, draw a surprise, step forward, repeat — the fan chart is that procedure run 1,000 times.

(2) The engine: how loud are the surprises? 3

Before any prior, the model needs to say how the surprises εt behave. The simplest assumption — same loudness always — is visibly wrong in this data:

Quiet mid-2000s, a financial-crisis hump, a Covid spike an order of magnitude larger. The engine models this with three learned parts, and it helps to read the equations bottom-up: (2c) says each variable's loudness drifts slowly; (2e) says a one-off freak quarter can be absorbed by a temporary multiplier instead; (2a) turns the 28 correlated surprises into 28 independent ones so each can have its own loudness.

(2a)
(2b)
(2c)
(2d)
(2e)

Read it as: B0 (2a) is the co-movement pattern — which surprises arrive together. Its prior (2d) centres it on the identity matrix (“start from: surprises don't co-move”) with the same looseness for every element, and that symmetry is the point: it is what makes the whole engine order-invariant — reshuffling the variable list cannot change any result. hi,t (2c) is shock i's loudness on a log scale; its priors say loudness is persistent (ρi near 0.95: this quarter's loudness is mostly last quarter's) and moves in small steps (the InvGamma keeps σ²η small); the hi,1 term just starts the path from its own long-run distribution rather than an arbitrary point. oi,t (2e) is the freak-quarter escape valve: normally exactly 1 (no effect); with small probability pout — held near 1% by the Beta(2,200) prior unless the data insist — it can jump to 2–20 and absorb a spike. 2020Q2 is fitted as a large o, not as a permanent rise in h and not by distorting the coefficients: that one mechanism is the entire Covid treatment. Note what the engine never touches: c and the B's. It only decides how loudly each quarter's evidence speaks when they are estimated.

ALTERNATIVES Homoskedastic (the fallback engine): delete (2b)–(2e) and set Σt = Σ, one constant covariance — simpler, but the fan chart cannot widen in turbulent times and 2020 must be handled by hand. Cholesky-SV (retired): keep everything but force B0 triangular — zeros above the diagonal mean variable 1's surprise may move variable 28's but never the reverse, so the variables are no longer treated symmetrically, and the forecast changed by ∼9% of GDP when the list was merely re-ordered. That asymmetry is exactly what (2d)'s symmetric prior removes.

(3) The coefficient prior: a default and a leash for every lag weight. 2

Discipline number one acts coefficient by coefficient. Every lag weight gets a Normal prior — a default value (its mean) and a leash (its variance, limiting how far data can pull it). The defaults encode “each variable is a random walk that ignores the others”; the leashes are computed by one formula:

(3a)
(3b)

Piece by piece: the indicator in (3a) puts the default at 1 on a variable's own first lag and 0 everywhere else — that IS the random walk. The 0.04 in (3b) is κ, the one tightness dial (a leash of sd 0.2 on first-lag weights). The 1/ℓ² makes leashes shorter on older lags — the model must be very sure before using year-old information. The σ²i/σ²m ratio converts units: σ²j is variable j's typical own-noise, measured once by a small AR(4) pre-regression per variable, so “one leash length” means the same thing whether the regressor is log-GDP or an interest rate. And the intercepts ci are left essentially free (leash 100σ² — a deliberate non-choice with consequences: see the DIO story in block 4). Here are the actual 112 leashes of the GDP equation, computed by this formula from this vintage's data, next to what estimation did with the own-lag defaults:

The heatmap teaches the formula: columns fade left to right (the 1/ℓ² decay), rows differ by the unit conversion (quiet series get short leashes as regressors, noisy ones long). The dot plot shows the outcome: own first-lag weights hug the δ=1 default, drifting only where 157 quarters of evidence argued — the prior holding unless the data object. One disambiguation before moving on, because three “sigma-like” objects are now in play: lower-case = one fixed calibration number per variable (this block only); capital = the estimated, time-varying covariance of the surprises (block 2); and what papers call is nothing but all the Vℓ(i,m) leashes filed into one matrix — implied by (3b), never estimated.

ALTERNATIVE Horseshoe (rejected): replace the fixed 0.04 with learned leashes — , where the global dial κ and a local scale Ψ for every single coefficient get their own priors and are estimated. The intent is to pin most coefficients hard while releasing the few that matter. On trending levels data the learned cross-variable κ collapsed toward zero — the model stopped letting any variable learn from any other — so production rowed back to the fixed leashes above.

(4) The behaviour prior: invented quarters appended to the data. 2

Discipline number two is a different machine entirely. Instead of restraining coefficients one at a time, it teaches the system behaviour — by fabricating a handful of extra data rows and stacking them under the real sample:

The production rows are the sum-of-coefficients (SOC) prior: one invented quarter per variable, in which variable i has sat at — its average over the sample's first four quarters — forever, with the intercept switched off. Written as the row actually fed to equation i:

(4a)

Stare at it: with the intercept zeroed, the only way the equation fits this row well is if the own-lag weights sum to one — full persistence, “a variable that has been stable stays put”. Nothing forces the fit; the row simply pulls, and dividing through by μ = 0.2 makes the row five times larger, hence — in a least-squares sense — much harder to ignore. Smaller μ would shout louder; μ = 1 would whisper. These 28 rows are why the estimated dynamics sit just above the unit root (the histogram in the charts section), and relaxing them was the root cause of two past failures. The PLR rows (4b, Theory-anchored variant only) are the same construction applied to combinations: pick a theory matrix H whose rows are the term spread, consumption − GDP, and so on, and append one row per combination holding Hiy at its presample average, at volume 1/φ (φ = 0.2). Setting H = identity recovers (4a) exactly — SOC is the special case “every variable is its own combination”. PLR says nothing about where any level should be; only that theory-stable combinations should not trend.

ALTERNATIVES DIO / single-unit-root (rejected) — ONE more invented quarter, for the whole system at once, with the intercept switched on:
(4c)
Compare with (4a): the loaded intercept is the only difference, and it is the whole point — this row can be fit either by unit-root dynamics or by a small intercept, so it disciplines drift that comes from the constant term (which (3a) left essentially free). Tested at every tightness θ from 0.02 to 1.0 on this data: it never binds, the marginal likelihood rejects it by ∼11 log points, and its adaptive variant hurt out-of-sample accuracy — see its card. Villani (mothballed) is not a row at all: it rewrites block (1) around an explicit steady state, , with a prior on itself — elegant where a steady state exists; trending log-levels have none, and it failed exactly that way.

(5) What estimation returns. 4

Bayes' rule multiplies the blocks together:

(5)

The left side is a probability distribution over every unknown at once — the ∼12,800 numbers in the first table below. No formula solves it, so it is sampled by a Gibbs sampler: draw each group of unknowns from its exact conditional distribution given the current values of all the others, and cycle — (i) the lag weights c, B given the engine's state; (ii) B0; (iii) the loudness paths h; (iv) ρ and σ²η; (v) the outlier scales o and pout — 10,000 sweeps, the first 5,000 discarded as warm-up, every 5th kept. What remains is 1,000 stored draws, each a complete, internally-consistent version of the model. Their agreement is what the model knows; their disagreement IS the parameter uncertainty; and everything downstream — fan charts, decompositions, the structural layer — is computed on all 1,000, never on a single “best” estimate.

Everything the sampler learns (the parameters)
ObjectSymbolHow many numbersIts prior
Lag weights & interceptsc, B1…B43,164eq. (3) — the Minnesota default-and-leash prior
Surprise co-movementB0784each element Normal(1 if i=j else 0, 1) — eq. (2d)
Loudness pathshi,t28 × 157 = 4,396the AR law (2c), started from its own long-run distribution
Loudness persistenceρi28Normal(0.95, 0.04) — eq. (2d)
Loudness step sizeσ²η,i28InvGamma(5, 0.04) — eq. (2d)
Outlier scalesoi,t28 × 157 = 4,396= 1 normally; Uniform{2…20} with prob pout — eq. (2e)
Outlier frequencypout1Beta(2, 200) — prior mean ≈ 1% — eq. (2e)
Total ≈ 12,800 learned numbers, against 157 × 28 ≈ 4,400 data points — the arithmetic fact that makes blocks (3) and (4) non-optional.
Every dial we set (the hyperparameters & design choices)
DialEq.What it controlsWhere decided
δ = 1(3a)the default: every variable a random walkcard: Prior mean
κown = κcross = 0.04(3b)leash length on lag weightscard: Minnesota tightness
κint = 100(3a)intercept leash — deliberately loose (≈ unrestricted)design (CKY convention)
1/ℓ² decay(3b)leashes shorten with squared lag distancedesign rule
σ²j(3b)28 unit-calibration constants, measured by per-variable AR(4) pre-regressions — not chosen, not estimated in the modelmeasured from data
μ = 0.2(4a)volume of the SOC invented quarterscard: Sum-of-coefficients
H, φ = 0.2(4b)the theory combinations and their volume — Theory-anchored variant onlycard: Theory-anchored (PLR)
ρ-prior (0.95, 0.04); InvGamma(5, 0.04); Beta(2, 200); grid {2…20}(2d–e)the engine's own dials — standard literature values (CKY / Stock-Watson), never tuned by usdesign (literature)
p = 4 lags; sample 1986Q1→(1)how much history each equation sees; where the data startcards: Sample start
The rule of thumb. A parameter is anything in the first table: estimation learns it, and it arrives as 1,000 draws. A hyperparameter is anything in the second: a dial we set before estimation that shapes what the model is allowed to believe. Every contested dial has a decision card further down recording why its value was chosen and what else was tried.

Inside stage 2 — the prior, piece by piece

Nearly everything at the prior stage is one of two machines. They attack different problems and do not overlap: channel A restrains individual coefficients; channel B teaches behaviour with invented evidence.
CHANNEL A — a belief about the coefficients
The model has ∼3,200 coefficients and only ∼160 quarters of data. Channel A handles that imbalance: every coefficient gets a default value and a leash. The data can pull a coefficient away from its default, but only as far as the leash allows. (This channel is equation (3) above; its dials are δ and κ.)
Prior mean δ = 1 PRODUCTION
The default itself. Each variable is presumed a random walk — “tomorrow ≈ today” — the right starting belief for trending levels data. Attacks: what should the model believe where the data are silent. (An ADF-adaptive δ that switched defaults per variable was tried and destabilised the long horizon.)
Tightness κ = 0.04 PRODUCTION
The leash length, shared by all coefficients: smaller = tighter hold on the default. Attacks: overfitting. 0.04 is the deliberate compromise — κ=0.08 forecast ∼2% better but made the variance decomposition implausible (each variable explained only by itself).
Lag decay (fixed design) PRODUCTION
Leashes shorten automatically with lag distance (a lag-squared rule), so the model must be very sure before it uses year-old information. Attacks: overfitting where it is worst — the many distant-lag coefficients.
Horseshoe (per-coefficient leashes) REJECTED
Would have let the data choose each leash individually (most coefficients pinned, a few released). Its global dial collapses on trending levels data — cross-variable learning shut down entirely. Rowed back to the fixed leashes above.
CHANNEL B — artificial observations added to the data
Channel B is different machinery: invent a handful of fake data rows that encode an economic belief, and stack them on top of the real sample. The estimation cannot tell them apart from data, so the belief competes with evidence on equal terms — a belief about behaviour, not about any single coefficient. (This channel is block (4) above; the rows are written out there exactly, and μ, φ, θ are their volume dials.)
Sum-of-coefficients μ = 0.2 PRODUCTION
Rows saying “a variable that has been stable stays put” — they discipline each variable's own persistence toward one. Attacks: long-run stability. This is the source of the near-unit-root posterior (the histogram above); relaxing it was the root cause of both the explosive-SV defect and the Villani failure.
PLR, φ = 0.2 (variant prior) PRODUCTION
Rows saying “combinations that theory calls stable should not trend” — the term spread, the consumption-to-GDP ratio — while each individual level stays free. Attacks: theory-free long-run drift in relationships. Run as a separate estimation and published as the Theory-anchored forecast object rather than folded into the baseline.
DIO / single-unit-root REJECTED
One row, built from the sample's opening values, taming the shared deterministic drift. Attacks: the same long-run problem as SOC, via the constant term. Examined three times: it never binds here at any plausible tightness, the evidence rejects it, and its adaptive variant hurt out-of-sample accuracy.
What deliberately is NOT in the prior. Two further disciplines were considered and placed elsewhere. Villani (mothballed) is neither channel — it rebuilds the whole model around an explicit steady state, which trending log-levels do not have. And the long-run anchors (CBO potential, EIU annual figures) could have been prior beliefs but are instead applied after estimation, at stage 5, by reweighting simulated futures: the prior and likelihood stay clean of institutional numbers, and the anchors can change every month without re-estimating the model. When the two channels pull in different directions — channel A holding each variable near a random walk, channel B insisting stable combinations stay stable — the balance is decided by the data through the ordinary Bayesian arithmetic; the dials (κ, μ, φ) set how loudly each side argues.

What comes out — the five forecast objects

Display nameInternalWhat it isModule
Model baselineForecast 1Pure unconditional posterior predictive under oi_svbvar/forecasting.py
Theory-anchoredForecast 5 (PLR)Long-run relationships in the prior (GLP PLR, φ=0.2); differs from the baseline by the prior onlybvar/plr.py
Benchmark-anchoredForecast 2Baseline + joint entropic tilt to CBO potential and FRED/FOMC long-run ratesbvar/longrun_anchors.py
Analyst-anchoredForecast 3 — the headlineEIU quarterly hard pins (Kalman smoother) + the 5-year annual-average tiltbvar/conditions.py
MF nowcast fold-inForecast 4 (BETA)Monthly mixed-frequency BVAR folded into the baseline's current quarter (--mf-nowcast)bvar/mfbvar.py
How each is produced and read (as published on the dashboard)

The forecast dashboard publishes a regularly updated forecast for the United States from a 28-variable Bayesian vector autoregression (BVAR), estimated on quarterly Federal Reserve Economic Data (FRED) back to 1986 with four lags.

The model uses order-invariant stochastic volatility (Chan-Koop-Yu 2024) on top of a fixed Minnesota coefficient prior and a sum-of-coefficients prior. The stochastic-volatility specification, which is outlier-robust, lets the width of the uncertainty bands respond to the macroeconomic environment rather than being fixed. The posterior is sampled by Gibbs (10,000 draws, 5,000 burn-in, thinned to 1,000).

The dashboard shows four forecasts that differ only in what is assumed, named by where each one's discipline comes from:

  • Model baseline (grey, headline). The model's own forecast with nothing imposed — the honest view of what the data and the estimated dynamics imply. It fits the short run well; because a levels VAR has no structure forcing convergence, its long horizon drifts (rates and inflation tend to climb), which is exactly what the anchored variants below are there to discipline.
  • Benchmark-anchored (navy). The model's own dynamics, with the long run gently steered toward published reference values — the Congressional Budget Office's potential growth and natural rate of unemployment, the FOMC's longer-run policy rate, and market-based inflation expectations — by entropic tilting at the five-year horizon. Nothing is imposed in the near term; the anchoring is a soft reweighting of the model's own paths, not a hard pin, so the bands stay smooth.
  • Theory-anchored (green). The same model with long-run economic relationships — spreads, expenditure shares, the output gap — built into the prior itself (a "priors for the long run" specification), so the long run is disciplined from inside the model rather than adjusted afterwards. No numeric target is imposed anywhere.
  • Analyst-anchored (red). Conditioned on a central analyst forecast for the near term — those values are imposed as hard quarterly pins through a Kalman filter and a Carter-Kohn smoother for roughly the first two years — and tilted to the analyst's annual averages in the long run.

Shaded areas are nested 68% and 90% credible bands, shown for one forecast at a time (hover or tap a chip above the charts to flip them); following central-bank fan-chart convention they fan out from the last observed data point.

The variance decomposition attributes each variable's forecast uncertainty to groups of structural shocks under a recursive ordering. This is a statistical model output, not advice; long-horizon interest-rate paths are the least reliable element and should be read as indicative.

Two charts that explain a lot

The model is estimated in log levels and every posterior draw's dynamics sit a hair above the stability boundary (median largest root 1.0225). This is the correct behaviour for trending macro data — and the reason the model reports rolling recent-window shock decompositions rather than whole-sample ones, and why long-run information enters through post-estimation tilts rather than in-likelihood priors (see the DIO card below).
The forecast objects are one posterior wearing four sets of information: nothing (baseline), long-run theory (theory-anchored), institutional long-run benchmarks (benchmark-anchored), and the analyst path (analyst-anchored — the headline).

Estimation & prior decisions

Each card: what production uses, what was considered, why, and the evidence pack. Badges: green = in production / defended, red = examined and rejected or rowed back, grey = retired/mothballed. The outlined chip is the pipeline stage the decision belongs to.

Volatility engine

PRODUCTION 3 · ENGINE
Chosen: Order-invariant stochastic volatility (Chan-Koop-Yu 2024) with an outlier-robust layer ('oi_sv')
Considered / rejected: Homoskedastic (kept as fallback); Cholesky-SV ('full_sv', retired from the CLI — ordering-dependent, ~9% GDP sensitivity to variable order, and carries the Bognanni-2022 equation-by-equation draw bias)
Why: Best GDP CRPS at every horizon; order-invariant; Covid-robust without dummies; SV carried through the conditional and nowcast layers.
Evidence: docs/engine_modes.md; docs/model_testing_and_decisions.md §2.1

Coefficient prior under SV

ROWED BACK 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: Fixed Minnesota (κ_own = κ_cross = 0.04, the textbook Litterman design)
Considered / rejected: CKY's horseshoe global-local prior (collapsed on levels data: cross-shrinkage → 0, FEVD degenerated to 100% own-shock) and the own-lag-horseshoe variant (same collapse, opposite direction)
Why: I(1) levels data breaks the horseshoe's global scale (Piironen-Vehtari 2017 mechanism); fixed Minnesota keeps the CRPS advantage with a balanced FEVD.
Evidence: docs/oisv_cross_prior_decision.md

Minnesota tightness κ = 0.04

PRODUCTION 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: κ = 0.04, confirmed set-optimal on the 28-variable set
Considered / rejected: κ = 0.08 (forecasts ~2% better but collapses the FEVD own-share to an implausible level)
Why: A forecast-defensible compromise that keeps the structural layer interpretable; flagged as ideally size-adaptive.
Evidence: docs/calibration/03, 07

Sum-of-coefficients prior μ = 0.2

PRODUCTION 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: SOC at 0.2 (inherited from the R original, interrogated and retained July 2026)
Considered / rejected: Removing or loosening it
Why: Underpins the near-unit-root stability; its removal was the root cause of the explosive-SV defect and the Villani failure.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/15; NOTES.md 2026-07-02

Single-unit-root (DIO) prior

REJECTED 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: Kept OUT of production (default-off research flag)
Considered / rejected: Sims (1993) dummy-initial-observation prior at any tightness θ ∈ [0.02, 1.0]
Why: Three separate examinations: no stabilising effect on the companion roots at any tightness (median root ~1.008 throughout); the marginal likelihood rejects a binding DIO by ~11 log points; the GLP size-adaptive variant over-shrinks long-horizon real activity out of sample. Long-run information lives in the post-hoc tilting layer instead.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/01_prior_ablation.md; calibration/02; econometrics/14

Prior mean: random walk (δ=1)

PRODUCTION 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: Random-walk-centred Minnesota prior mean
Considered / rejected: ADF-adaptive prior mean ('rejected, catastrophically')
Why: The levels system wants the unit-root centring; the adaptive variant destabilised the long horizon.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/13, 14

Covid handling

PRODUCTION 3 · ENGINE
Chosen: Outlier-robust SV absorbs 2020 (production); Lenza-Primiceri observation downweighting (covid_scale) available on the structural/homoskedastic path
Considered / rejected: Covid dummy variables (the BoE route)
Why: The outlier layer handles the tail without changing the deterministic component; structural decompositions must footnote that 2020 re-identifies as huge shocks.
Evidence: docs/engine_modes.md; NOTES.md

Sample start 1986Q1

PRODUCTION 1 · DATA
Chosen: 1986Q1 (forced by two late-starting variables: EPU, Cleveland Fed inflation expectations)
Considered / rejected: 1974/1976 starts (used in structural experiments)
Why: The cost is documented: excluding the Volcker disinflation is why recursive monetary identification attenuates on the production sample; the 1976+/market-rate variant recovers the textbook contraction (--volcker).
Evidence: docs/calibration/06; docs/econometrics/03

Long-run anchoring: tilt-only

PRODUCTION 5 · FORECAST LAYER
Chosen: Entropic annual-average tilts for every variable (joint solver)
Considered / rejected: Hard anchor-pins at the 5-year quarter for rates (rowed back: produced the visible 'diabolo' — band collapsing to a point then re-widening)
Why: The pin was a stabiliser for the old mildly explosive homoskedastic engine; under oi_sv it is unnecessary and the tilt matches the annual-average data semantics.
Evidence: CLAUDE.md D6; docs/three_forecast_layers.md

Tilting solver

PRODUCTION 5 · FORECAST LAYER
Chosen: Joint entropic solver across the ~5 correlated long-run constraints
Considered / rejected: Sequential composition (strained ESS when constraints interact)
Why: Healthy effective sample sizes with all constraints met simultaneously.
Evidence: docs/three_forecast_layers.md

Villani steady-state BVAR

MOTHBALLED 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: Levels + I(1) dynamics + post-hoc tilts
Considered / rejected: Villani (2009) steady-state prior parameterisation
Why: Requires a stationary demeaned VAR; anchoring an I(1) log-level via a deterministic trend is out of domain — the unconditional forecast exploded once SOC was relaxed.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/villani_postmortem.md; mothballed/villani/

Theory-anchored forecast (PLR φ=0.2)

PRODUCTION 2 · PRIOR
Chosen: GLP-2019 'plausibility of long-run relationships' prior at SOC-equivalent tightness φ=0.2 — the 5th forecast object
Considered / rejected: φ=1 (median companion root 1.057, too loud)
Why: A clean one-factor comparison against the baseline: same model, long-run theory added in the prior.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/10, 15-18; docs/design/plr_forecast_object.md

Constant parameters

DEFENDED 3 · ENGINE
Chosen: Constant-parameter BVAR (TVP not adopted)
Considered / rejected: Time-varying-parameter variants
Why: The SV layer captures the volatility instability that motivates most TVP use; parameter drift not supported at this sample size.
Evidence: docs/econometrics/11_constant_params_defence.md

Named-shock menu: TIERA_9

PRODUCTION 6 · STRUCTURAL LAYER
Chosen: Nine named shocks (NAMED_7 + Housing/Credit + Policy Uncertainty) via CMY-minimal sign + ranking restrictions — 15 ranking cells, max 3 per shock; the Financial/Uncertainty pair separated by a moment-matched EPU-spread combination, with the combined F+U share always reported
Considered / rejected: The 24-cell ranking battery (every demand-type shock ranked on every separator — individually alive, jointly dead at ~4e-9 acceptance); dynamic 1:4 restrictions (139x throughput collapse for one block, CMY Table 3); an Uhlig-agnostic monetary column; an External shock without a foreign-activity variable
Why: Each probe stage passed its gates at CMY-class budgets (per-ranking-cell cost measured at 1.8-2.0x); the design follows CMY (2025) Table 4's separation-by-distinguishing-variables principle rather than ranking batteries.
Evidence: docs/research/tier_design_2026-07.md; shock_menu_2026-07.md

Tier B research model: 32 variables, NAMED_10

CANDIDATE 6 · STRUCTURAL LAYER
Chosen: A 32-variable research variant (production 28 + labor productivity, real hourly compensation, real federal receipts, house prices) carrying a 10-shock menu: the generic supply shock split into Technology / Labour Supply / Wage Bargaining by wage- and unemployment-sign restrictions alone, plus a Tax shock disciplined by a receipts variable on every cyclical column (Mountford-Uhlig). ON ICE: results displayed for the record; promotion awaits a full toolkit pass (conditioning, anchoring, validation, backtest).
Considered / rejected: The 12-shock endgame at current budgets (measured 4 acceptances per 5M rotations — every shock column individually alive, jointly beyond the budget class; needs pooled 20M+ or importance sampling); ULCNFB (spanned); a bank-lending-standards variable (sample starts 1990); a foreign-activity variable (no vintage-clean series)
Why: The 10-shock probe passed decisively (765 acceptances per 5M, 310 unique draws) and its posterior mirrors production (companion max-root median 1.023 vs 1.023); the measured acceptance ratio between the 10- and 12-shock menus (191 = 1.93^8) validated the programme's cost arithmetic.
Evidence: docs/research/tierb32_candidate_2026-07.md; tier_design_2026-07.md

Structural identification set

PRODUCTION 6 · STRUCTURAL LAYER
Chosen: Four tools — CEGSQ conditional (recursive), sign-restricted SBVAR (incl. NAMED_7SHOCK with ranking restrictions), Baumeister-Hamilton (oil), external instruments (proxy-IV) — plus the named-shock decomposition layer (BoE idiom)
Considered / rejected: Raw-instrument proxy SVAR as the production oil ID (demand-contaminated at quarterly frequency, retired Apr 2026, confirmed July 2026); NAMED_9 extension (separability-required ranking battery collapses acceptance — recorded negative result)
Why: Each primitive is used only where it is well identified; the decision guide lives in docs/structural_tools.md.
Evidence: docs/structural_tools.md

Variable-set variants

SetStatusNotes
big_financial (28 vars)PRODUCTION since 2026-06the 24-var CBO set + Baa-10Y spread, housing starts, NASDAQ, trade-weighted USD; promoted on identification (correct risk-off sign), forecast-equivalent
original (24 vars)rollback (--candidate original)the CBO/Yoo set; prior production
combined / financial / oil_supply (25-28)tested, not adoptedcalibration-program candidates; the early 'more vars = worse' result was substantially a prior artifact
23var-oil / 25var (1974+)structural experiments onlylonger-sample oil-identification sets (Blanchard attenuation, SBVAR)
The calibration arc in one paragraph: a naive post-Covid screen said “add oil and financial variables”; the honest real-time, DM-tested re-run said “keep the incumbent”; a further pass showed that negative result was substantially a prior artifact; and big_financial was then promoted on identification grounds (the credit spread carries the correct risk-off sign) while remaining forecast-equivalent. Evidence: docs/calibration/00–08.

How the model got here

The development arc from the CBO base to the current production model — the big changes only, each argued in the evidence packs linked from the cards above.
2023-08
The methodology base
CBO Working Paper 2023-08 (Yoo): a 24-variable Bayesian VAR in log levels with Minnesota + sum-of-coefficients priors — the design this model descends from.
2026-01
R implementation
The 24-variable R model (us_bvar): Kalman-smoother conditional forecasting on an analyst baseline, mixed-frequency handling, scenario machinery.
2026-02
Python port
Full reimplementation on free FRED data with an R-match test suite; EIU baselines move from Excel to the API with local caching.
2026-03
The tilting layer
Entropic tilting added as the long-run anchoring mechanism — reweighting simulated paths after estimation rather than putting long-run dogma in the prior. First external econometric review.
2026-04
Structural toolkit, round 1
Sign-restricted identification (SBVAR) added; the raw-instrument proxy SVAR tried and retired (demand-contaminated at quarterly frequency); peer review and cleanup.
2026-05
Production hardening
Economic-validation release gates, golden-master tests, the production dashboard, and a full-model review.
2026-06
The engine month
Order-invariant stochastic volatility adopted as the production engine; its horseshoe coefficient prior collapsed on levels data and was rowed back to a fixed Minnesota (κ=0.04). The 28-variable big_financial set promoted. Hard rate pins replaced by tilt-only anchoring (removing the 'diabolo' band artifact).
2026-07
Five forecast objects; structural suite, round 2
The PLR Theory-anchored forecast ships as the fifth object; the monthly mixed-frequency nowcast folds into the current quarter; joint tilting replaces sequential. External-instrument monetary identification, seven named shocks with ranking restrictions, the BoE-style decomposition layer, and the FEVD coverage toolkit (block shares + max-share) join the standing toolkit.
Source: FRED. Joshua Bailey Bayesian VAR · vintage 2026-07
